content:
Member-only story

5 min read
Just now

--

TL;DR: MCP is an open standard for connecting AI to external tools/data — the “USB-C” for AI. It works with a Host (app) + Client + Server architecture; servers expose Tools/Resources/Prompts and turn the M×N integration mess into M+N. You can add a server to Claude Desktop via config in minutes, and inspect any server with the MCP Inspector.
Hello, lately the abbreviation “MCP” shows up everywhere; but most articles start in the middle, leaving you wondering “what exactly is this?” 🤔. I wrote this guide to go through it once, in a clean order: first what it is and how it works, then we’ll run an MCP server on your machine and inspect it with the MCP Inspector, and finally wrap up with a real example. Ready? Let’s go.
What Is MCP?
MCP — the Model Context Protocol — is an open-source standard Anthropic introduced in late 2024. In one sentence: a common language for connecting AI applications to external systems — files, databases, services.
Why was it needed? Because every model had to be wired to every tool by hand; that’s the “M × N” problem (M models × N tools = a pile of custom integrations). MCP brings it to “M + N”: write the tool once as an MCP server, every model uses it. As the official docs put it, MCP is a kind of “USB-C” for AI 😉 — one plug, fits everywhere.
